explanation: Isododecane is a lightweight, volatile isoparaffinic hydrocarbon used as a fast-evaporating solvent and emollient that imparts exceptional spreadability and a clean, dry skin feel with no residue. It is non-comedogenic, well-tolerated across skin types, and widely used in long-wear foundations and lip products for its ability to suspend pigments and deliver actives while leaving a weightless finish.

Isoeugenol is a phenylpropanoid fragrance compound found in ylang ylang, carnation, and many other essential oils, classified as one of 26 known fragrance allergens required to be disclosed by name on EU cosmetic labels. It is among the more potent fragrance sensitizers — capable of triggering allergic contact dermatitis and an itch-and-scratch cycle on reactive or fragrance-sensitive skin — and is best avoided in leave-on products for sensitive skin.

Isohexadecane is a lightweight, volatile isoparaffinic hydrocarbon emollient providing excellent spreadability and a clean, non-greasy skin feel. It is non-comedogenic, well-tolerated across skin types, and used in foundations, sunscreens, and lightweight moisturizers as a skin-feel enhancer and pigment dispersant.

Isononyl isononanoate is a lightweight branched-chain ester emollient derived from isononanoic acid, providing a dry, silky skin feel with good spreadability and no greasy residue. It is well-tolerated, non-comedogenic, and used in lightweight moisturizers and sunscreens as a comfortable skin-feel enhancer.

Isopropyl alcohol (IPA) is a fast-evaporating solvent used in skincare to dissolve active ingredients and deliver a quick-drying sensation. At concentrations used in toners and astringents, it can disrupt the skin barrier, strip natural lipids, and cause dryness and irritation — particularly on reactive, dry, or barrier-compromised skin — making it one of the more contested ingredients in modern skincare formulation.

Isopropyl isostearate is a branched-chain ester emollient derived from isopropyl alcohol and isostearic acid, providing a smooth, emollient skin feel in moisturizers and lip formulas. It has moderate comedogenic potential and is generally well-tolerated, though its occlusive nature may promote congestion in acne-prone or breakout-susceptible skin at higher concentrations.

Isopropyl lauroyl sarcosinate is an amino acid-derived ester emollient providing a comfortable, non-greasy skin feel with mild conditioning properties. It is well-tolerated, biodegradable, and used in natural-positioned and skin-compatible formulas as a lightweight emollient and mild surfactant.

Isopropyl myristate is a widely used synthetic ester emollient derived from myristic acid and isopropyl alcohol, providing a lightweight, spreadable texture with rapid skin absorption. It has well-documented moderate-to-high comedogenic potential and is best avoided in formulas targeting acne-prone or congestion-prone skin types.

Isopropyl myristate is a widely used synthetic ester emollient providing a lightweight, spreadable texture with rapid skin absorption. It has well-documented moderate-to-high comedogenic potential and is best avoided in formulas targeting acne-prone or congestion-prone skin types.

Isopropyl palmitate is a synthetic ester emollient derived from palmitic acid and isopropyl alcohol, providing a light, smooth skin feel in moisturizers and foundations. It has moderate comedogenic potential and may promote congestion in acne-prone or breakout-susceptible skin at higher concentrations.

Isopropyl palmitate is a synthetic ester emollient providing a light, smooth skin feel in moisturizers and foundations. It has moderate comedogenic potential and may promote congestion in acne-prone or breakout-susceptible skin at higher concentrations.

Isopropyl shea butterate is an ester derived from isopropyl alcohol and shea butter fatty acids, providing a lightweight, non-greasy emollient skin feel with better spreadability than raw shea butter. It is well-tolerated and used in moisturizers and lip products for its comfortable texture and shea-derived fatty acid conditioning.

Isopropyl titanium triisostearate is a titanium ester used primarily as a coupling agent and dispersant to improve the dispersion of mineral particles — particularly titanium dioxide and zinc oxide — in sunscreen and foundation formulas for more even pigment distribution. It is considered well-tolerated and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic use concentrations.

Isotridecyl isononanoate is a lightweight branched-chain ester emollient providing a dry, silky skin feel with excellent spreadability and no greasy residue. It is well-tolerated, non-comedogenic, and used in lightweight moisturizers and sunscreens as a comfortable skin-feel enhancer.

Jasminum officinale (jasmine) flower extract provides polyphenol antioxidants and skin-conditioning compounds with free-radical-scavenging properties. The extract form is generally well-tolerated, though individuals with fragrance sensitivity should note that jasmine extracts can contain naturally occurring fragrance compounds — patch-testing is advisable for reactive skin.

Jojoba esters are the naturally occurring wax esters from Simmondsia chinensis (jojoba) seed oil — a mixture of long-chain fatty acids and alcohols that structurally resembles human sebum, enabling excellent skin compatibility and non-comedogenic emollient properties. They are exceptionally well-tolerated across all skin types including oily and acne-prone, and used in moisturizers and lip products for their biomimetic skin-feel and conditioning.

Juniperus communis (juniper berry) fruit oil is an essential oil containing alpha-pinene, sabinene, and terpene compounds with a characteristic woody-fresh scent and documented contact sensitization risk. It can trigger allergic contact dermatitis and an itch-and-scratch cycle on reactive or fragrance-sensitive skin and is best avoided in leave-on products for sensitive skin.

Juniperus mexicana (Texas cedarwood) oil is an essential oil containing cedrol and cedrene terpenes with a woody fragrance and documented contact sensitization risk. It can trigger allergic contact dermatitis and an itch-and-scratch cycle on reactive or fragrance-sensitive skin, particularly in leave-on products.

Kaolin is a fine white clay mineral (aluminum silicate) used in face masks, cleansers, and powders as a gentle absorbent that binds excess sebum and impurities from the skin surface without significantly stripping natural oils. It is the gentlest of the cosmetic clays and exceptionally well-tolerated — suitable for sensitive, dry, and normal skin types where stronger clays such as bentonite would be too stripping.

Lactic acid is a mild AHA exfoliant derived from fermentation that accelerates cell turnover and resurfaces skin texture while simultaneously functioning as a humectant — making it gentler than glycolic acid due to its larger molecular size and dual hydrating action. At effective concentrations it can cause stinging, tingling, and temporary redness on reactive or barrier-compromised skin, and significantly increases UV sensitivity, requiring strict daily sun protection.

Lactobacillus ferment is produced by culturing Lactobacillus bacteria, delivering postbiotic metabolites — including lactic acid, peptides, and exopolysaccharides — that support skin microbiome balance and strengthen the skin barrier. It is well-tolerated, non-sensitizing, and used in calming and microbiome-focused formulas for its ability to reduce skin sensitivity and support a healthy microbial environment.

Lactobacillus ferment lysate consists of broken-down Lactobacillus bacterial cell contents, delivering postbiotic fragments including peptides and structural components that reinforce the skin's barrier function and microbiome defense. It is well-tolerated and used in sensitive skin and microbiome-focused formulas for its soothing and barrier-reinforcing properties.

Lactobacillus — the asterisk indicates organic certification — is a probiotic bacteria genus used in skincare in ferment or lysate form to deliver postbiotic metabolites that support the skin microbiome and reinforce barrier function. It is well-tolerated and used in microbiome-focused and calming formulas.

Lactobacillus — the asterisk indicates organic certification — is a probiotic bacteria genus used in skincare in ferment or lysate form to deliver postbiotic metabolites that support the skin microbiome and reinforce barrier function. It is well-tolerated and used in microbiome-focused and calming formulas.

Lactobacillus/Eriodictyon californicum (yerba santa) ferment extract combines probiotic-derived postbiotic metabolites with polyphenol antioxidants from fermented yerba santa, delivering both microbiome-supporting and free-radical-scavenging benefits in a single ingredient. It is well-tolerated and used in antioxidant and microbiome-supportive formulas.

Lactobacillus/pumpkin ferment extract is produced by fermenting pumpkin with Lactobacillus bacteria, combining probiotic-derived postbiotics with carotenoid and enzyme antioxidants from pumpkin for enhanced bioavailability. It is well-tolerated and used in antioxidant and gentle resurfacing formulas for its combined postbiotic and plant antioxidant content.

Lactobacillus/soybean ferment extract is produced by fermenting soybean with Lactobacillus bacteria, delivering bioavailable isoflavone derivatives and postbiotic metabolites with antioxidant and skin-conditioning properties. It is generally well-tolerated, though individuals with soy sensitivity should note the botanical source.

Lactococcus/hyaluronic acid ferment filtrate is produced by fermenting hyaluronic acid with Lactococcus bacteria, yielding a biofermented HA complex with postbiotic metabolites that may enhance moisture retention and skin-soothing properties beyond standard HA. It is well-tolerated and used in advanced hydrating and microbiome-supportive formulas.

Ultramarines (CI 77007) are synthetic mineral pigments derived from sodium aluminum sulfosilicate, used in cosmetics to provide blue, pink, and violet tones in eyeshadows, face powders, and color cosmetics. They are considered inert, non-sensitizing, and well-tolerated, though restricted from use in lip products in some jurisdictions due to sulfide impurity concerns.

Laminaria digitata (kelp) extract is a brown seaweed botanical rich in fucoidan, alginate polysaccharides, iodine, and antioxidant polyphenols with hydrating, antioxidant, and skin-soothing properties. It is well-tolerated and used in marine-ingredient and protective formulas for its broad-spectrum antioxidant and moisture-supporting content.
